video_title: Everything Is Outperforming the S&P 500 This Year | Animal Spirits 470
gist: Through late June 2026 nearly every equity category (Russell 2000, small/large-cap value, REITs, midcaps, EM, dividends) is beating an S&P 500 that is itself up ~10%, because the MAG7 are in deep drawdowns as the market punishes their capex-heavy AI buildout. The hosts dissect a narrow semiconductor mania (everyone is overweight), conflicting investor-positioning data, AI-driven business formation, the collapse of self-help books and Ethereum, and prediction-market scandals.

insights.claim: Through the Monday close in late June 2026, the Russell 2000, small-cap value, large-cap value, REITs, midcaps, emerging markets and dividend stocks were all outperforming an S&P 500 that was itself up roughly 10% for the year, an outcome few thought possible without the S&P falling.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Diversification away from the S&P 500 cap-weighted index is working even in an up-market year, favoring value, small-cap and international tilts over MAG7 concentration.
insights.quote: Everything is outperforming the S&P 500 this year.

insights.claim: The MAG7 are not participating in the 2026 rally and are keeping a lid on S&P 500 returns, with Microsoft down 33%, Meta down nearly 30%, Oracle nearly 50% off highs, Netflix off 45%, Google down 13%, Amazon down 15%, and Tesla down 17%.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: The market's biggest weights are in correction-to-bear territory simultaneously, so the equal-weight index and the '493' are the place to be while megacap leadership is broken.

insights.claim: The market is rerating the hyperscalers because they have shifted from asset-light free-cash-flow machines to asset-heavy capex spenders pouring nearly all free cash flow into data centers, eroding the 'invincibility' premium their stocks carried.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Watch capex-to-FCF ratios and margin trajectory at the megacaps; the multiple compression is fundamentally driven and may persist as long as spending stays elevated.
insights.quote: Asset light was good. Asset heavy not so good.

insights.claim: The average PE for Microsoft, Amazon, Google and one other megacap is at its lowest level in five years, with Microsoft trading like the world's largest software stock being disrupted by AI rather than a diversified franchise.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Compressed megacap multiples could be a setup for value buyers if the AI-disruption-of-software thesis on names like Microsoft proves overstated.

insights.claim: By going into the physical world and building data centers, the hyperscalers may have disrupted themselves and made their margins less defensible, with the rest of the economy positioned as the beneficiary of that capital spend.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Frames a long-the-beneficiaries (suppliers, semis, broader economy), short-the-spenders trade as megacap intangibility erodes.
insights.quote: We view not participating as an existential threat. We have no choice.

insights.claim: Founder-CEOs with large ownership stakes like Mark Zuckerberg can absorb 30% drawdowns without altering AI-capex strategy, so Meta-style spending is unlikely to be reined in by stock-price pressure alone.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Don't expect founder-led megacaps to cut AI capex in response to share-price weakness; the spending overhang on margins can persist.

insights.claim: Hedge-fund net exposure to semiconductors has gone parabolic, rising from 8% at the start of 2026 to 22%, while retail is piling into semi ETFs and the DRAM ETF doubled from $10 billion to $20 billion in a week.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Semis are the single most crowded trade across both professional and retail money, raising the risk of violent air-pocket drawdowns on any disappointment.
insights.quote: It has gone from 8% at the beginning of the year to 22% today.

insights.claim: Semiconductor trailing-12-month net profit margins are projected to rise from a 2024 low near 25% to 49% by 2027, a structural margin expansion driving the sector's earnings and stock gains.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: The semi rally is backed by a forecast doubling of margins, not just multiple expansion, but 49% margins invite competitive entry or mean-reversion risk.

insights.claim: Semiconductor supply cannot be brought to market quickly because cutting-edge fabs like Taiwan Semi's are extraordinarily hard to replicate, creating persistent bottlenecks across the entire supply chain.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Structural supply scarcity supports semiconductor pricing power and the bull case even at high valuations, while the bottleneck favors incumbents over would-be competitors.
insights.quote: You can't just bring supply to the market. You can't just snap your fingers and fix the issue.

insights.claim: In the past 12 months 42 stocks in the Morningstar large-mid index more than doubled (over twice the 10-year average) and 12 doubled in the last six months, with SK Hynix up over 300% year-to-date, mostly tied to the AI-infrastructure boom.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: The breadth of 100%+ movers signals a hot AI-hardware cohort where outsized gains imply equally fast, expected profit-taking flushes.

insights.claim: Given the size of semiconductor gains, Micron could fall 30% in a week (as it did in March) and still remain in a strong uptrend, so violent shakeouts should be treated as normal rather than thesis-breaking.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Sizing and stomach for ~30% drawdowns are prerequisites for owning the high-momentum semis; the shakeouts are as fast as the rallies.
insights.quote: Micron could fall 30% in a week and still be in an incredibly strong uptrend.

insights.claim: Goldman Sachs research shows equity valuations (CAPE) have followed return on equity higher, justifying elevated multiples on fundamentals rather than pure speculation.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Bearish high-CAPE arguments lose force when ROE is structurally higher; judge the PE against the underlying ROE before calling the market expensive.

insights.claim: Return on equity, historically one of the most cyclical series in finance and pegged by Buffett at a stable 12-14%, has shifted to a permanently higher level this decade because technology rewrote the economics of incremental invested capital.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: A durable step-up in aggregate ROE supports higher equilibrium equity valuations, but the same dynamic is now reversing for capex-heavy hyperscalers.
insights.quote: These companies that we've discussed at Nauseium rewrote the physics laws of business of what we thought was possible.

insights.claim: The day of SpaceX's IPO marked the largest single day of net retail stock buying ever recorded by Citadel Securities (which handles ~35% of US retail volume), and nine of its ten largest trading days ever occurred in the prior month, signaling a retail euphoria spike.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Record retail trading concentrated around the SpaceX listing is a froth signal, even if it doesn't pin down net direction.

insights.claim: Deutsche Bank shows consolidated equity positioning at only the 42nd percentile (discretionary 41st, systematic 40th), and Bloomberg shows speculators getting more net short futures, contradicting the retail-mania narrative.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Aggregate positioning is far from crowded, leaving room for upside even amid pockets of euphoria; the bull case has dry powder.
insights.quote: Equity positioning sits in the 42nd percentile. Still far from crowded, leaving plenty of room for further upside.

insights.claim: Investor-behavior charts are noisy enough to support any narrative, so investors should weigh who is presenting a chart and what story they are pushing rather than treating the data as decisive.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: A discipline for filtering sentiment data: consider the source's incentive and narrative before acting on any single positioning chart.
insights.quote: It's very easy to make any case based on any of these charts.

insights.claim: Household cash as a percentage of total financial assets is now 8%, the highest since 1990, which Carlson attributes mainly to baby boomers rotating out of bonds into cash now that it yields 3-4%, rather than to broad bearishness.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Elevated cash is demographic/yield-driven, not a coiled-spring buy signal nor a contradiction of the speculative pockets in the market.
insights.quote: Household cash as a percentage of total financial assets is now 8%.

insights.claim: An AI sentiment survey found only 8% of investors believe there is no bubble in AI-related stocks while 51% say some but not all AI stocks are too expensive, meaning persistent doubt rather than universal belief characterizes AI sentiment.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Widespread bubble fear means there are still skeptics to convert into buyers, arguing against a classic euphoric top in AI for now.
insights.quote: 51% of people said some but not all AI related stocks are too expensively valued.

insights.claim: Since the start of the Middle East war, gold is down ~20% and oil has round-tripped to only +10% while the semiconductor index is up ~70%, showing semis hedged a war better than the traditional gold/oil hedges.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Conventional war hedges failed; there are no perfect hedges for every scenario, undercutting reflexive gold/oil positioning into geopolitical events.
insights.quote: Gold's down 20% since the start of the war which is kind of crazy.

insights.claim: There has been no 'normal' market or housing environment this century, so models that project a reversion to a normal regime are flawed because no such baseline exists.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Investors should stop anchoring to a mythical normal and instead treat large, fast moves as the prevailing regime.
insights.quote: There's no such thing as normal. Everything is every market environment is a little bit different.

insights.claim: Stripe Economics (Ernie Tedeschi) finds US new business applications have more than doubled, with the current acceleration driven by AI lowering the cost of starting a business rather than by any government program like the earlier PPP-era surge.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: AI as a tailwind to small-business creation is a durable, under-appreciated bullish signal for the broader economy and SMB-exposed plays.

insights.claim: The key investing question for AI is shifting from which tasks it replaces to which previously impossible tasks it enables, illustrated by an insurance firm now able to analyze 200-300 annuity policies and prospectuses instantly that it could never have reviewed before.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Reframes AI value creation toward net-new capability and expanded TAM, favoring AI-enabled service firms rather than only labor-replacement plays.
insights.quote: What task is AI going to allow you to do that you couldn't do before.

insights.claim: Tim Ferriss's catalog will sell roughly 80% fewer print copies in 2026 than in 2022, with self-help books being displaced by AI assistants like Claude that can answer any question on demand.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: A concrete early example of AI disrupting information-product publishing; watch for similar displacement across books, courses, and how-to media.
insights.quote: His catalog will sell roughly 80% fewer print copies in 2026 than it did in 2022.

insights.claim: Despite a perceived compute shortage, Gavin Baker notes less than 3% of the world is actually using AI, so demand is at the earliest stage of adoption.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: A long runway for AI adoption supports the structural-demand bull case for compute and semis even at elevated valuations.

insights.claim: Bank of America data shows total card spending rose 5.1% year-over-year in May 2026, the strongest growth in nearly four years, indicating remarkably robust consumer spending momentum (partly aided by gasoline).
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Resilient consumer spending supports a soft-landing / no-recession backdrop favorable to consumer-exposed equities.
insights.quote: Total card spending rising 5.1% year-over-year in May the strongest growth in nearly four years.

insights.claim: Credit card issuers can simultaneously charge ~20-25% APRs and offer 0% promotional financing for 18-21 months because the high-rate revolvers subsidize the promotional balances, a structure Batnick frames as effectively transferring cost from prime to subprime borrowers.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Highlights the cross-subsidy in consumer credit; savvy prime borrowers can perpetually roll 0% offers while subprime APRs fund it.
insights.quote: Because the 25 pay for the zero.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley data shows housing affordability (monthly payment as a percentage of income) is now back to roughly 2004-2007 and 1994 levels because incomes have risen, but low transaction activity and tight supply keep buying difficult.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Affordability has quietly normalized via income growth, but frozen activity and limited inventory are the binding constraints on housing-related plays.

insights.claim: Zillow's market cap has collapsed from $48 billion in the 2021 mania to under $7 billion and the stock is ~85% off its all-time high, despite a strong consumer brand.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Brand strength has not protected Zillow against frozen housing-transaction volumes; a cautionary case on betting on a housing turn via the stock.

insights.claim: The latest Social Security trustees report projects the trust fund can pay 100% of benefits through 2032-33, dropping to 83% in 2034 and 65% by 2100, and the hosts expect the government to fund the gap with debt rather than cut benefits.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: If shortfalls are debt-financed rather than benefit-cut, it adds to fiscal-deficit and Treasury-supply pressure over the long term.

insights.claim: Ethereum has round-tripped back near its ~$1,400 2017 highs despite years of hype around smart contracts 'owning the internet,' which Carlson cites as a case of widely-followed thinkers simply being wrong.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: A reminder that consensus 'smartest people' crypto theses can fail to translate into token returns; demand explanations, not just narratives.
insights.quote: What happened to this thing being like, I'm going to own the internet.

insights.claim: Crypto's real momentum is in blockchain infrastructure and stablecoins rather than tokens, with Visa down 12% and Mastercard multiples compressing on stablecoin-disruption fears, and ICE/NYSE partnering with OKX (co-chaired by Andrew Cuomo) to tokenize NYSE-listed assets.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: The actionable crypto trade may be shorting payment-network multiples on stablecoin disruption and watching tokenization-of-securities ventures, not buying tokens.
insights.quote: Crypto is having a moment. It's just the blockchain and not the tokens.

insights.claim: Prediction-market volume has collapsed from over 90% politics/economics two years ago to under 6% today, with the remainder dominated by sports and parlays, undermining the original 'bet on real outcomes' use case.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Prediction markets have become de facto sportsbooks, raising the question of what moat they hold against DraftKings/FanDuel beyond regulatory positioning.
insights.quote: Two years ago, prediction market volume was over 90% politics and economics. Today, these same categories are under 6% of total volume.

insights.claim: The durable moat for prediction-market firms is regulatory and lobbying spend (tens of millions on legal and lobbyists) rather than product, leaving them exposed if administrations or rules change.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Regulatory capture as a moat is fragile to political turnover, a key risk for valuations of prediction-market platforms.

insights.claim: A Wall Street Journal investigation found Polymarket's contractor paid influencers to post fake winning bets: of 118 videos by 10 creators depicting ~$900,000 in wins, the bets ($1.9 million in total) were not real and would actually have lost over $166,000.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Documented fake-influencer marketing is a reputational and regulatory red flag for Polymarket and the prediction-market category.

insights.claim: Insider-trading scandals are hitting prediction markets, with Kalshi planning to require users to disclose employers after military-spouse accounts accurately bet on Maduro's ouster days early and a Google employee was charged for betting over $1 million on most-searched-people outcomes using non-public data.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Insider-trading enforcement and new disclosure rules add compliance friction and headline risk to the prediction-market business model.

insights.claim: Both hosts argue the 2021 crypto/NFT episode was a far more extreme speculative mania than the current 2026 environment, which they view as more grounded in real earnings and margins despite a speculative element.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Framing the current AI/semi run as fundamentals-backed rather than 2021-style froth argues against an imminent broad-market crash even if pockets correct.
insights.quote: I think that that period was way more of a speculative mania than the current one. And I don't think it's close.

insights.claim: It is notable that the 2021 mania's collapse stayed contained to crypto and speculative stocks without spilling into the broad market, whereas a comparable AI blow-up today would likely not stay limited to a ~20% market decline given AI's weight.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Highlights concentration risk: an AI unwind would be far more systemic than the contained 2021 crypto crash, a tail risk for broad-index holders.
